# 10.1 Chrome DevTools

> Chrome DevTools is the primary debugging and profiling environment for web development -- knowing it deeply turns hours of guesswork into minutes of targeted investigation.

---

## Opening DevTools

```
F12                    -- toggle DevTools
Ctrl+Shift+I           -- toggle DevTools (alternative)
Ctrl+Shift+J           -- open directly to Console
Ctrl+Shift+C           -- open and activate element picker (inspect mode)
```

On macOS, replace `Ctrl` with `Cmd` and `Shift` remains the same.

DevTools can dock to the right, bottom, or left side of the browser, or undock into a separate window. Use `Ctrl+Shift+D` to cycle dock positions.

---

## Elements Panel

The Elements panel shows the live DOM tree and computed styles. It is the primary tool for inspecting and modifying HTML structure and CSS in real time.

### Inspecting elements

```
Ctrl+Shift+C           -- activate the element picker
                          click any element on the page to jump to it in the DOM tree
```

Once an element is selected:
- The right pane shows **Styles** (all CSS rules affecting this element, in specificity order)
- **Computed** shows the final resolved values after the cascade
- **Layout** shows Flexbox/Grid overlays and box model dimensions
- **Event Listeners** shows JavaScript event handlers attached to this element

### Live editing

Double-click any attribute, tag name, or text content in the DOM tree to edit it. Changes apply immediately in the page.

```
# Edit CSS directly in the Styles pane:
# Click on any property value to change it
# Click the empty space after the last property to add a new one
# Use Up/Down arrow keys to increment/decrement numeric values by 1
# Hold Shift for increments of 10
# Hold Alt for increments of 0.1
# Hold Ctrl for increments of 100
```

### Force element states

Right-click an element in the DOM tree and select "Force state" to simulate:
- `:hover`
- `:active`
- `:focus`
- `:focus-within`
- `:focus-visible`
- `:visited`
- `:target`

This keeps the state active so you can inspect hover styles without holding the mouse in position.

### DOM breakpoints

Right-click an element and select "Break on..." to pause JavaScript execution when:
- **Subtree modifications:** a child node is added or removed
- **Attribute modifications:** any attribute of this element changes
- **Node removal:** this element is removed from the DOM

DOM breakpoints are essential for tracking down code that dynamically modifies elements you are investigating.

### Accessibility inspection

The Elements panel includes an **Accessibility** tab showing:
- ARIA attributes and computed role
- Accessible name and description
- Contrast ratio for text elements (with pass/fail for WCAG AA/AAA)
- Full accessibility tree view

### The `$0` shortcut

The currently selected element in the Elements panel is available in the Console as `$0`. Previous selections are `$1`, `$2`, up to `$4`.

```javascript
// In the Console, after selecting an element in Elements:
$0                     // the selected DOM node
$0.textContent         // its text content
$0.classList           // its CSS classes
$0.getBoundingClientRect()  // its position and size
```

---

## Console Panel

The Console is both an output for log messages and an interactive JavaScript REPL.

### Console API beyond `console.log`

```javascript
// Styled output
console.log('%cImportant message', 'color: red; font-size: 20px; font-weight: bold');

// Table format for arrays/objects
const users = [
  { name: 'Alice', role: 'admin', active: true },
  { name: 'Bob', role: 'editor', active: false },
  { name: 'Carol', role: 'viewer', active: true },
];
console.table(users);
// Renders a formatted table in the console

// Selective columns
console.table(users, ['name', 'role']);

// Grouped output
console.group('Authentication Flow');
console.log('Step 1: Check token');
console.log('Step 2: Validate permissions');
console.warn('Step 3: Token expired, refreshing...');
console.groupEnd();

// Collapsed group (useful for verbose logging)
console.groupCollapsed('Detailed request data');
console.log(requestHeaders);
console.log(responseBody);
console.groupEnd();

// Timing
console.time('API call');
await fetch('/api/data');
console.timeEnd('API call');
// Output: API call: 342.5ms

// Count occurrences
function handleClick(type) {
  console.count(type);  // logs: "submit: 1", "submit: 2", etc.
}

// Assertion (logs only when the condition is false)
console.assert(user !== null, 'User should exist at this point', { userId });
// If user is null: Assertion failed: User should exist at this point {userId: 42}

// Stack trace
console.trace('Where was this called from?');

// Clear the console
console.clear();
```

### Console utilities (only available in Console, not in code)

```javascript
// Query selector shorthand
$('nav')               // document.querySelector('nav')
$$('.card')            // Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('.card'))

// XPath queries
$x('//div[@class="container"]')

// Copy to clipboard
copy($0.outerHTML)     // copy selected element's HTML
copy(someObject)       // copy a JS object as JSON string

// Monitor function calls
monitor(someFunction)  // logs every call to someFunction with arguments
unmonitor(someFunction)

// Monitor events on an element
monitorEvents($0, 'click')     // log all click events on selected element
monitorEvents(window, 'resize')
unmonitorEvents($0)

// Get all event listeners for an element
getEventListeners($0)
// Returns: {click: Array(2), mouseenter: Array(1), ...}

// Last evaluated expression
$_                     // value of the last expression evaluated in Console

// Keys and values of an object
keys(someObject)       // Object.keys(someObject)
values(someObject)     // Object.values(someObject)
```

### Filtering console output

The Console toolbar has filters:
- **Errors**, **Warnings**, **Info**, **Verbose** toggle buttons
- Text filter: type a string to show only messages containing it
- URL filter: filter by the source file URL
- Regex filter: prefix with `/` for regex matching

### Live expressions

Click the "eye" icon in the Console toolbar to create a live expression. It evaluates a JavaScript expression continuously and shows the current value, updating in real time. Useful for watching a value without cluttering the console with repeated logs.

```javascript
// Live expressions examples:
document.querySelector('.modal').classList.value
performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize / 1024 / 1024
document.querySelectorAll('img:not([alt])').length
```

---

## Sources Panel: JavaScript Debugging

The Sources panel is a full JavaScript debugger.

### Setting breakpoints

Click the line number in any JavaScript file to set a breakpoint. When execution reaches that line, it pauses.

Types of breakpoints:

| Type | How to set | When it pauses |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| Line | Click line number | At that specific line |
| Conditional | Right-click line > "Add conditional breakpoint" | When condition is `true` |
| Logpoint | Right-click line > "Add logpoint" | Never pauses; logs a message instead |
| DOM | Elements panel > "Break on..." | When DOM changes |
| XHR/Fetch | Sources > XHR/fetch Breakpoints | When a request URL matches a pattern |
| Event Listener | Sources > Event Listener Breakpoints | On any event type (click, keydown, etc.) |
| Exception | Click pause icon in Sources panel | On any thrown exception (caught or uncaught) |

### Conditional breakpoints

Pause only when a specific condition is true. This avoids breaking on every iteration of a loop:

```javascript
// In a loop processing 1,000 items, you only care about item 42:
// Right-click the line number > Add conditional breakpoint:
// Condition: item.id === 42
for (const item of items) {
  processItem(item);  // breakpoint here, condition: item.id === 42
}
```

### Logpoints

Logpoints log to the console without pausing execution and without modifying source code. They are the DevTools equivalent of adding a temporary `console.log` that you never have to remember to remove.

```
// Right-click line number > Add logpoint
// Message: "Processing item:", item.id, "total:", items.length
// This appears in the console each time the line executes
```

### Debugger controls

Once paused at a breakpoint:

| Button | Keyboard | Action |
|--------|----------|--------|
| Resume | `F8` | Continue to next breakpoint or end |
| Step Over | `F10` | Execute current line, move to next line |
| Step Into | `F11` | Enter the function being called |
| Step Out | `Shift+F11` | Execute rest of current function, return to caller |
| Step | `F9` | Step to the next statement (including async steps) |

### The Scope pane

When paused, the **Scope** pane on the right shows all variables visible at the current execution point:
- **Local:** variables in the current function
- **Closure:** captured closure variables
- **Script:** module-level variables
- **Global:** `window` / global scope

### The Call Stack pane

Shows the chain of function calls that led to the current execution point. Click any frame to jump to that call site and see its local variables.

### Watch expressions

Add any JavaScript expression to the **Watch** pane. It is re-evaluated at every breakpoint pause. Useful for monitoring computed values or object properties.

```
// Watch expression examples:
user.permissions.includes('admin')
cart.items.reduce((sum, i) => sum + i.price, 0)
response.headers.get('content-type')
```

### The `debugger` statement

Adding `debugger;` in your source code acts as a programmatic breakpoint:

```javascript
function processPayment(amount) {
  if (amount > 10000) {
    debugger;  // pause here only for large transactions
  }
  // ...
}
```

Remove `debugger` statements before committing. ESLint's `no-debugger` rule catches these automatically.

### Blackboxing (Ignore List)

Library code (React internals, lodash, etc.) clutters the call stack and breakpoints. Right-click a script in the Call Stack or Sources tree and select "Add to ignore list." DevTools will skip over these frames during step-through debugging.

The Settings panel (`F1` > Ignore List) lets you configure patterns:

```
# Patterns to ignore:
/node_modules/
webpack-internal:///
.*\.min\.js$
```

### Source maps

Source maps connect minified/bundled production code back to the original source files. When source maps are available (`.map` files), DevTools automatically shows the original TypeScript, SASS, or unminified JavaScript in the Sources panel.

If source maps are not loading:
1. Verify `.map` files are deployed alongside the bundles
2. Check that the bundle contains `//# sourceMappingURL=bundle.js.map`
3. Open DevTools Settings > Preferences > "Enable JavaScript source maps"

---

## Network Panel

The Network panel records all HTTP requests made by the page.

### Key columns

| Column | What it shows |
|--------|---------------|
| Name | Request URL (filename) |
| Status | HTTP status code |
| Type | Resource type (fetch, script, stylesheet, img, etc.) |
| Initiator | What triggered the request (script, parser, redirect) |
| Size | Transfer size vs actual size (shows compression savings) |
| Time | Total request duration |
| Waterfall | Visual timeline of the request phases |

### Filtering requests

```
# Filter bar syntax:
domain:api.example.com          -- requests to a specific domain
is:running                      -- in-progress requests
larger-than:1M                  -- requests larger than 1MB
status-code:404                 -- only 404 responses
has-response-header:cache-control  -- responses with a specific header
method:POST                     -- only POST requests
-domain:cdn.example.com         -- exclude a domain (negate with -)
```

Type filters: All, Fetch/XHR, JS, CSS, Img, Media, Font, Doc, WS, Wasm, Manifest, Other.

### Inspecting a request

Click any request to see its details:
- **Headers:** request and response headers, query parameters
- **Payload:** POST body, form data, or JSON payload
- **Preview:** parsed response (rendered JSON, image preview, HTML)
- **Response:** raw response body
- **Timing:** DNS, TCP, TLS, TTFB, content download breakdown
- **Cookies:** sent and received cookies

### Timing waterfall breakdown

```
Queueing        -- waiting in the browser queue
Stalled         -- waiting for a socket (browser limits 6 TCP connections per domain)
DNS Lookup      -- resolving the hostname
Initial Connection -- TCP handshake
SSL             -- TLS negotiation
Request Sent    -- uploading the request body
Waiting (TTFB)  -- time to first byte from the server
Content Download -- downloading the response body
```

A long **TTFB** means the server is slow. A long **Content Download** means the response is too large or the connection is slow.

### Throttling

The Network panel offers network throttling to simulate slow connections:
- Fast 3G, Slow 3G, Offline
- Custom profiles: define download/upload speed and latency

Enable "Disable cache" to simulate a first-time visitor experience.

### Copy as cURL

Right-click any request and select "Copy > Copy as cURL." This gives you a ready-to-paste terminal command that reproduces the exact request, including headers and cookies. Invaluable for reproducing API issues outside the browser.

### Block requests

Right-click a request and select "Block request URL" or "Block request domain." Useful for testing:
- What happens if a CDN fails?
- What happens if a third-party script does not load?
- What happens if an API returns no data (combine with blocking + error simulation)?

---

## Performance Panel

The Performance panel records a timeline of everything happening in the browser: JavaScript execution, rendering, painting, and network activity.

### Recording a profile

1. Open the Performance panel
2. Click the Record button (or `Ctrl+E`)
3. Interact with the page (the actions you want to profile)
4. Click Stop

DevTools displays:
- **CPU chart:** colored blocks showing what the CPU was doing
- **Frames:** frame rate over time (green = 60fps, red = dropped frames)
- **Main thread:** flame chart showing JavaScript call stacks
- **Network:** request timeline within the recorded period

### Reading the flame chart

The flame chart shows function calls stacked from bottom (entry point) to top (deepest call). Width represents time: wider blocks = longer execution.

```
Colors:
  Yellow  -- JavaScript execution (scripting)
  Purple  -- Rendering (style calculation, layout)
  Green   -- Painting (compositing, drawing pixels)
  Gray    -- System / idle time
```

Look for:
- **Long yellow blocks:** JavaScript is blocking the main thread
- **Purple blocks during interaction:** layout thrashing
- **Many narrow purple blocks in sequence:** forced synchronous layouts

### Live Metrics (Core Web Vitals)

The Performance panel now includes a **Live Metrics** view that shows real-time Core Web Vitals:
- **LCP (Largest Contentful Paint):** when the main content appears
- **INP (Interaction to Next Paint):** responsiveness to user input
- **CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift):** visual stability

This view updates continuously as you interact with the page, without needing to start/stop a recording.

### Identifying layout thrashing

Layout thrashing occurs when JavaScript reads a layout property (like `offsetHeight`), forces the browser to calculate layout, then writes to the DOM, then reads again:

```javascript
// BAD: layout thrashing
// Each iteration reads offsetHeight (forces layout), then writes style.height
for (const el of elements) {
  el.style.height = el.offsetHeight + 10 + 'px';  // read-write-read-write loop
}

// GOOD: batch reads, then batch writes
const heights = elements.map(el => el.offsetHeight);  // read all at once
elements.forEach((el, i) => {
  el.style.height = heights[i] + 10 + 'px';           // write all at once
});
```

In the Performance panel, layout thrashing appears as a dense series of purple "Layout" events interleaved with yellow "Script" events.

---

## Memory Panel

The Memory panel helps find memory leaks and excessive memory usage.

### Three profiling types

| Type | Use case |
|------|----------|
| **Heap snapshot** | Point-in-time snapshot of memory allocation. Compare two snapshots to find leaks. |
| **Allocation timeline** | Shows memory allocation over time. Identifies what code is allocating memory. |
| **Allocation sampling** | Lightweight sampling profiler. Lower overhead, less detail. |

### Finding memory leaks with heap snapshots

The standard technique for finding a memory leak:

1. **Take Snapshot 1** (baseline)
2. **Perform the action** that you suspect leaks (e.g., open and close a modal 10 times)
3. **Take Snapshot 2**
4. **Compare:** select Snapshot 2, change the view to "Comparison" and compare against Snapshot 1
5. **Look for objects that should have been garbage-collected** but still exist

```
# In the comparison view:
# Delta column shows the count difference
# If "Detached HTMLDivElement" has +50 in Delta, you are leaking DOM nodes

# "Retained Size" shows how much memory would be freed if an object
# were garbage-collected. Large retained sizes indicate significant leaks.
```

### Common memory leak patterns

```javascript
// Leak 1: Event listeners not removed
function setupDialog() {
  const dialog = document.createElement('div');
  document.body.appendChild(dialog);

  // This listener keeps `dialog` alive even after removeChild
  function handleKeydown(e) {
    if (e.key === 'Escape') dialog.close();
  }
  document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeydown);

  // FIX: use AbortController to clean up
  const controller = new AbortController();
  document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeydown, { signal: controller.signal });
  // When closing: controller.abort();
}

// Leak 2: Closures holding references
function createHandler(largeData) {
  // `largeData` is captured by the closure and never freed
  // even if the handler only uses `largeData.id`
  return function handler() {
    console.log(largeData.id);
  };
}
// FIX: extract only what you need before creating the closure
function createHandler(largeData) {
  const id = largeData.id;
  return function handler() {
    console.log(id);
  };
}

// Leak 3: Timers and intervals not cleared
const intervalId = setInterval(() => {
  const el = document.getElementById('status');
  if (el) el.textContent = Date.now();
  // If `el` is removed from the DOM, the interval keeps running,
  // and the closure keeps a reference to the stale element.
}, 1000);
// FIX: clearInterval(intervalId) when the component unmounts
```

---

## Application Panel

The Application panel inspects client-side storage:

| Storage type | Inspect | Edit | Delete |
|---|---|---|---|
| localStorage | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| sessionStorage | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cookies | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| IndexedDB | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cache Storage | Yes | No | Yes |
| Service Workers | Register/Unregister | No | Yes |

### Useful operations

- **Clear site data:** Application > Storage > "Clear site data" button. Wipes everything: localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, cookies, service workers, cache.
- **Edit cookies:** Double-click any cookie value to modify it. Useful for testing different authentication states.
- **Inspect service workers:** See registered workers, push event logs, and manually trigger sync/push events.

---

## Lighthouse Panel

Lighthouse runs automated audits and generates reports for:
- **Performance:** loading speed, interactivity, visual stability
- **Accessibility:** ARIA, contrast, keyboard navigation
- **Best Practices:** HTTPS, console errors, deprecated APIs
- **SEO:** meta tags, crawlability, mobile-friendliness

```
# Run Lighthouse:
1. Open Lighthouse panel
2. Select categories to audit
3. Click "Analyze page load"
4. Review the report (scores 0-100 per category)
5. Expand individual items for fix suggestions
```

Lighthouse runs in a simulated throttled environment by default. Scores may differ from real-world performance.

---

## Common Mistakes

- **Using `console.log` for everything instead of breakpoints.** Adding 20 `console.log` statements to trace execution is slower and less informative than setting a single breakpoint and using the Scope and Call Stack panes. Breakpoints show all variables at once without modifying code.

- **Not using conditional breakpoints in loops.** Pausing on every iteration of a 1,000-item loop to find the one bad item wastes time. A conditional breakpoint with `item.id === 42` gets you directly to the problem.

- **Ignoring the Network panel timing waterfall.** Developers see "the API is slow" but do not check whether the slowness is DNS, TTFB, or content download. The timing breakdown pinpoints the exact phase.

- **Leaving `debugger` statements in committed code.** Always configure ESLint's `no-debugger` rule to catch these in CI.

- **Not using source maps in development.** Debugging minified code is orders of magnitude harder. Ensure your build tool generates source maps for development builds.

- **Taking only one heap snapshot.** A single snapshot tells you how much memory is used, not whether it is leaking. You need two snapshots (before and after an action) and the comparison view to find leaks.

- **Misreading the Performance panel colors.** Yellow is scripting, purple is rendering, green is painting. A purple block during a click handler means layout was triggered inside JavaScript (layout thrashing), not that the CSS is slow.

- **Testing performance without throttling.** Your development machine is faster than most user devices. Use CPU throttling (4x or 6x slowdown) in the Performance panel to simulate real-world conditions.

---

## Best Practices

- **Learn the keyboard shortcuts.** `Ctrl+Shift+C` (inspect), `F8` (resume), `F10` (step over), `F11` (step into) are the core workflow. Memorize them.

- **Use logpoints instead of adding temporary `console.log` in source.** Logpoints achieve the same result without modifying code and without having to remember to clean up.

- **Use the "Preserve log" checkbox in Console and Network panels.** Without it, page navigation clears the log. Enable it when debugging redirect chains or multi-page flows.

- **Use `copy()` to extract data.** `copy(JSON.stringify(someObject, null, 2))` copies a nicely formatted JSON string to your clipboard from the Console.

- **Profile performance with CPU throttling enabled.** 4x slowdown in the Performance panel simulates a mid-range mobile device. Problems invisible on your workstation become obvious.

- **Add scripts to the Ignore List.** Blackbox third-party libraries and bundler runtime code. This makes the call stack readable and prevents stepping into framework internals.

- **Use the "Block request" feature for resilience testing.** Block your CDN domain and verify the application degrades gracefully. Block an API endpoint and verify error handling works.

---

## Real-World Use Cases

### Diagnosing a slow API response

A page takes 4 seconds to render after clicking "Load Reports."

```
1. Open Network panel, clear existing entries
2. Click "Load Reports"
3. Find the /api/reports request
4. Click it, open the Timing tab:
   - DNS: 0ms (cached)
   - TTFB: 3,200ms  <-- the server is slow
   - Content Download: 150ms
5. Conclusion: the backend query needs optimization, not the frontend.
   Share the TTFB timing with the backend team.
```

### Finding a DOM memory leak

Users report the page gets slow after navigating between tabs in a single-page application.

```
1. Open Memory panel
2. Take Snapshot 1
3. Navigate between tabs 20 times
4. Take Snapshot 2
5. Select Snapshot 2, choose "Comparison" view, compare to Snapshot 1
6. Sort by Delta (descending)
7. Find: "Detached HTMLDivElement" Delta: +200
   -- 200 DOM nodes are being created but never garbage-collected
8. Expand to see retainers (what is keeping these nodes alive)
9. A closure in a setInterval callback holds a reference to the removed tab element
10. Fix: clear the interval when the tab is destroyed
```

### Debugging a layout shift

The page's CLS score is 0.35 (bad). An element is visually jumping after load.

```
1. Open Performance panel
2. Enable "Screenshots" (checkbox at top)
3. Record a page load
4. In the timeline, look for "Layout Shift" events (marked in the Experience track)
5. Click a Layout Shift event to see which elements moved
6. DevTools highlights the shifted element and shows the shift distance
7. Check the screenshots filmstrip to see exactly when the shift happened
8. In this case: an ad banner loads without a reserved height, pushing content down
9. Fix: add explicit width/height or aspect-ratio to the banner container
```

---

> **Key Takeaway:** Stop scattering `console.log` across your code -- conditional breakpoints, logpoints, the Network timing waterfall, and heap snapshot comparisons provide deeper insight faster and without modifying a single line of source.
